results.title: Strait of Hormuz Remains Near-Empty With Just A Few Iran Ships Moving
results.author: Bloomberg
results.text:
Strait of Hormuz Remains Near-Empty With Just A Few Iran Ships Moving

The Epaminondas ship is seen during seizure by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) in the Strait of Hormuz, Iran, in this image obtained by Reuters on April 24, 2026. Meysam Mirzadeh/Tasnim/WANA (West Asia News Agency) via REUTERS

# Strait of Hormuz Remains Near-Empty With Just A Few Iran Ships Moving

By Prejula Prem and Julian Lee

Apr 25, 2026 (Bloomberg) –The [Strait of Hormuz](https://gcaptain.com/three-vessels-hit-by-gunfire-in-strait-of-hormuz-crews-safe/) remains largely empty of merchant ships, with only a few Tehran-linked vessels moving through the waterway, following a tense week that saw Iranian gunboat attacks and tanker interceptions by the US Navy.

As of Saturday morning, observable traffic was down to only two very small fuel carriers and one tiny coastal cargo ship, all with ties to Tehran, leaving the Persian Gulf through the crucial waterway, vessel-tracking data compiled by Bloomberg show. No ships were observed on their way in.

While Iran continues to fill supertankers with millions of barrels, the US Navy has stalled several of them near the maritime border with Pakistan before
highlights:
Apr 25, 2026 (Bloomberg) –The Strait of Hormuz remains largely empty of merchant ships, with only a few Tehran-linked vessels moving through the waterway, following a tense week that saw Iranian gunboat attacks and tanker interceptions by the US Navy.
[...]
As of Saturday morning, observable traffic was down to only two very small fuel carriers and one tiny coastal cargo ship, all with ties to Tehran, leaving the Persian Gulf through the crucial waterway, vessel-tracking data compiled by Bloomberg show. No ships were observed on their way in.
[...]
While Iran continues to fill supertankers with millions of barrels, the US Navy has stalled several of them near the maritime border with Pakistan before they can leave the Gulf of Oman into the Arabian Sea, creating a bottleneck that underscores the growing difficulties for the Islamic Republic’s exports.
[...]
Vessels transiting Hormuz with active Automatic Identification System signals during the past day were confined to a narrow northern lane near the Iranian islands of Larak and Qeshm, the route approved by Tehran.
[...]
The US blockade may encourage Iran-linked ships entering or leaving the Persian Gulf to switch off their tracking signals to avoid detection, making it harder to get an accurate picture of traffic through the waterway. This means transit figures may sometimes be revised higher when vessels reappear far away from the riskiest waters.
[...]
It was common, even before the US imposed its latest restrictions, for Iran-linked ships to stop sending signals as they headed into the Strait of Hormuz to exit the Persian Gulf. They generally didn’t enable them again until well into the Strait of Malacca in South East Asia, about 13 days sailing from Iran’s Kharg Island.
[...]
Because vessels can move without transmitting their location until they’re well away from Hormuz, automated positioning signals were compiled over a large area covering the Gulf of Oman, the Arabian Sea and the Red Sea to detect those that may have departed or entered the Persian Gulf.
[...]
Some transits may not have been detected if vessels’ transponders haven’t been switched back on. Iran-linked oil tankers often steam from the Persian Gulf without broadcasting signals until they reach the Strait of Malacca about 10 days after passing Fujairah in the UAE. Other ships may be adopting similar tactics and won’t show up on tracking screens for many days.
[...]
classes of commercial
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz remains largely quiet for merchant traffic, with only a few Iran-linked vessels moving and no observed inbound ships as of the latest data.
- Observable activity is confined to a narrow northern lane near Larak and Qeshm, reflecting Tehran’s control and a de facto traffic bottleneck created by US naval interdictions of several supertankers exporting Iranian oil.
- Iran is still loading supertankers, but US sanctions and naval presence are constraining outbound flows, heightening the strategic importance of Hormuz as a choke point.
- The US naval blockade and sanctions target Iranian oil buyers (e.g., Hengli Petrochemical), aiming to disrupt Iran’s export revenue and pressure negotiations.
- Tracking challenges remain: many Iran-linked vessels disable AIS to avoid detection, complicating accurate traffic assessments and watchstanding, potentially underrepresenting true transit volumes.
- Operational takeaway for watchstanders: expect intermittent, low-volume traffic through a narrow northern corridor; anticipate spoofing or signal-off events; maintain vigilance for sudden convoy movements or escalations tied to US-Iran tensions that could rapidly alter risk in the strait.

results.title: UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf
results.author: Dryad Global
results.text:
UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf

[Maritime Risk Intelligence Blog](https://channel16.dryadglobal.com/)

# UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf

Written by [Dryad Global](https://channel16.dryadglobal.com/author/dryad-global)| March 1, 2026 at 8:00 PM

On 01 March 2026, UKMTO issued Update 002 to Advisory 003-26, highlighting a highly volatile maritime security environment in the Arabian Gulf (also known as Persian Gulf), Gulf of Oman, North Arabian Sea, Bab al Mandab (BAM), and the Strait of Hormuz due to significant ongoing regional military activity.

This includes a substantial military presence contributing to elevated threats to commercial shipping, with risks of miscalculation or misidentification near military units, sensitive maritime facilities, and infrastructure. Mariners face potential for VHF hailing, directed communications from military units, and significant GNSS/electronic interference (including disruptions to AIS, positioning, navigation, and communications systems), which may be intermittent and unpredictable.

Claims of closure or transit restr
highlights:
On 01 March 2026, UKMTO issued Update 002 to Advisory 003-26, highlighting a highly volatile maritime security environment in the Arabian Gulf (also known as Persian Gulf), Gulf of Oman, North Arabian Sea, Bab al Mandab (BAM), and the Strait of Hormuz due to significant ongoing regional military activity.
[...]
This includes a substantial military presence contributing to elevated threats to commercial shipping, with risks of miscalculation or misidentification near military units, sensitive maritime facilities, and infrastructure. Mariners face potential for VHF hailing, directed communications from military units, and significant GNSS/electronic interference (including disruptions to AIS, positioning, navigation, and communications systems), which may be intermittent and unpredictable.
[...]
Claims of closure or transit restrictions in the Strait of Hormuz continue to circulate via open source reporting and VHF, but no official closure has been communicated through recognized channels such as NAVAREA warnings or IMO broadcasts; such VHF indications do not constitute a legal suspension of transit passage under UNCLOS. Broader regional instability extends threats beyond the Strait of Hormuz, including elevated tensions in the Bab al Mandab. JMIC guidance urges continuous VHF Channel 16 watch, AIS transmission per policy, adherence to Traffic Separation Schemes and corridors, professional responses to hails, enhanced risk assessments (considering military activity and insurance), and immediate reporting of unusual activity, suspicious approaches, interference, or incidents to UKMTO or other centers. The situation is under close monitoring with further updates expected as warranted.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The UKMTO advisory (003-26, Update 002) warns of a highly volatile security environment across the Strait of Hormuz, Bab al Mandab, Gulf of Oman, and surrounding Arabian Gulf areas due to ongoing regional military activity.
- Key risks for watchstanders at the Strait of Hormuz:
  - Elevated threat of miscalculation or misidentification near military units and sensitive facilities.
  - Increased likelihood of naval hails and directed communications from military assets.
  - Potential GNSS/electronic interference (AIS, positioning, navigation, communications) with intermittent, unpredictable disruption.
  - Reports of proposed or circulating vessel restrictions or closures in the Strait, though no official UNCLOS-backed closure has been issued by NAVAREA/IMO channels.
- Operational implications for watchstanders:
  - Maintain continuous VHF Channel 16 watch; monitor AIS per policy.
  - Adhere to Traffic Separation Schemes and recommended routing corridors; respond professionally to hails.
  - Conduct heightened risk assessments that account for ongoing military activity and potential insurance implications.
  - Report unusual approaches, interference, or incidents to UKMTO or appropriate maritime authorities immediately.
- Guidance and next steps:
  - Stay informed via UKMTO updates and Channel 16, and prepare operator-ready actions for evolving risks at chokepoints.
  - Expect possible changes in routing and increased vigilance when approaching the Strait of Hormuz.

results.title: Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns
results.author: Mike Schuler
results.text:
Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns

FILE PHOTO: Tankers are seen off the coast of the Fujairah, as Iran vows to close the Strait of Hormuz, amid the U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran, in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, March 3, 2026. REUTERS/Amr Alfiky/File Photo

# Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns

Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed to a near standstill as missile attacks, drone threats, and widespread electronic interference continue to [rattle the region](https://gcaptain.com/tag/iran-conflict/), according to the latest maritime security advisory from the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC).

In its latest update, JMIC said the regional maritime threat environment remains highly volatile amid the escalating conflict involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, warning that the risk to merchant shipping across the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and northern Arabian Sea remains “CRITICAL,” meaning an attack is considered almost certain.

While the strait technically remains open to navigation, vessel movements have collapse
highlights:
Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed to a near standstill as missile attacks, drone threats, and widespread electronic interference continue to rattle the region, according to the latest maritime security advisory from the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC).
[...]
In its latest update, JMIC said the regional maritime threat environment remains highly volatile amid the escalating conflict involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, warning that the risk to merchant shipping across the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and northern Arabian Sea remains “CRITICAL,” meaning an attack is considered almost certain.
[...]
While the strait technically remains open to navigation, vessel movements have collapsed as shipowners and charterers reassess the risks of transiting one of the world’s most important maritime chokepoints.A review of vessel tracking data cited in the advisory shows that traffic through the waterway has nearly stopped.
[...]
“Recent AIS review indicates transits have reduced to single-digit levels, with only one confirmed commercial transit observed in the past 24 hours,” the advisory said.
[...]
Data from MarineTraffic.com shows tanker traffic through Strait of Hormuz down by more than 90%. “Unlike several other vessel segments where movements have largely ceased, some tankers are still traveling east and west through the strait, with a number of voyages occurring under AIS blackouts,” says Matt Wright, Principal Freight Analyst at Kpler, parent company to MarineTraffic.com.
[...]
A containership transiting the Strait
[...]
region. The
[...]
is one of
[...]
across the region in
[...]
The JMIC advisory also highlighted widespread GPS and GNSS interference affecting vessels operating in the region, including AIS anomalies, positional inaccuracies, and degraded communications systems. Shipmasters were urged to closely monitor navigation equipment and be prepared to rely on radar, visual bearings, and traditional navigation techniques if satellite-based systems become unreliable.
[...]
that the maritime security environment could
[...]
missile activity expands closer to major shipping routes or if strikes occur against ports, offshore infrastructure
[...]
or terminals supporting Gulf energy exports
[...]
Shipping companies operating in the region have been advised to conduct enhanced voyage risk assessments before entering the Gulf, maintain reporting links with the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) center and the Maritime Security Centre – Indian Ocean (MSCIO), and follow established Best Management Practices for maritime security.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz remains a highly volatile maritime security hotspot due to ongoing US–Israel–Iran conflict, with advisory agencies warning the risk to merchant shipping is “CRITICAL” (almost certain attack).
- Current conditions: traffic through the strait has collapsed to near standstill, with AIS data showing single-digit transits and some vessels traveling under AIS blackout; the waterway is not formally closed, but routine commercial traffic has largely paused.
- Strategic importance: Hormuz is a critical global energy chokepoint, handling about 20% of world oil and significant LNG exports from Gulf producers.
- Operational impact for watchstanders:
  - Expect heightened threat environment: missile and drone attacks, projectile strikes, GPS/GNSS interference, AIS anomalies, and degraded communications.
  - Increased need for resilience: rely on radar, visual bearings, and traditional navigation methods if GNSS/satellite systems fail; maintain heightened situational awareness of vessel movements and potential collateral damage risks.
  - Navigation planning: anticipate restricted throughput and possible sudden changes in advisories or threat levels; prepare for rapid decision-making and potential rerouting.
- Notable incidents: recent vessel attacks (including a containership struck by a projectile) illustrate ongoing risks to ships, crews, and cargo in the area.
- Guidance for watchstanders: closely monitor navigation equipment, be prepared to switch to non-satellite navigation, and maintain readiness for security-related procedures and contingency plans.

results.title: - Cyprus Shipping News
results.author: adonis
results.text:
- Cyprus Shipping News

# Maritime security update: Gulf Region / Strait of Hormuz and Red Sea

[Top News](https://cyprusshippingnews.com/category/top-news/), [World](https://cyprusshippingnews.com/category/world/)

March 5, 2026

### The Wider Gulf Region: Maritime security update

Since yesterday, several merchant vessels were struck in or near the approaches to the Strait of Hormuz. In the latest Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) update the following incidents have been reported:

- SKYLIGHT (IMO 9330020) was reported hit about 5 nm north of Khasab (Oman) with four crew injuries and an evacuation.
- MKD VYOM (IMO 9284386) suffered a projectile strike above the waterline with a fire brought under control.
- An attack on SEA LA DONNA (IMO 9380532) is under investigation.

These incidents mark a shift from warnings to confirmed damage on multiple ships over the last 24–48 hours.

Dryad Global has also assessed that sea mines remain a credible escalation option for Iran because even limited, quickly laid fields can produce disproportionate disruption in a narrow lane such as Hormuz – largely by eroding confidence and forcing slower, controlled movements, as seen during the Ir
highlights:
Since yesterday, several merchant vessels were struck in or near the approaches to the Strait of Hormuz. In the latest Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) update the following incidents have been reported:
[...]
- SKYLIGHT (IMO 9330020) was reported hit about 5 nm north of Khasab (Oman) with four crew injuries and an evacuation.
[...]
- MKD VYOM (IMO 9284386) suffered a projectile strike above the waterline with a fire brought under control.
[...]
- An attack on SEA LA DONNA (IMO 9380532) is under investigation.
[...]
- US MARAD Maritime Alert 2026‑001A remains in force: avoid the wider Hormuz/Gulf of Oman/North Arabian Sea area where possible and keep ≥ 30 nm clear of US naval units; maintain contact with NCAGS and monitor official outputs. This alert is consistent with the heightened risk now evidenced by the recent strikes.
[...]
- UKMTO advisories continue to emphasise caution, reporting, and awareness of electronic interference and misidentification risks; masters should keep a listening watch on VHF 16 and report abnormal activity promptly.
[...]
The maritime risk picture has escalated markedly. The Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) now assesses the regional maritime threat as CRITICAL (an attack is almost inevitable) after confirmed missile/projectile strikes on multiple merchant vessels in the Gulf of Oman/Strait of Hormuz approaches within the past 24 hours. The UKMTO continues to describe the wider area as highly volatile, warning of sustained GNSS/AIS/VHF interference and noting that VHF claims of a “closure” of the Strait are unverified and not legally binding. In parallel, the U.S. MARAD Maritime Alert 2026‑001A advises vessels to avoid the region where possible and to maintain at least 30 nm separation from US naval units while remaining in contact with NCAGS and monitoring UKMTO/JMIC outputs.
[...]
300
[...]
Khasab
[...]
Although many ships have received VHF hails asserting that the Strait is closed, there is no formal legal closure: JMIC notes no NAVAREA warning, no IMO Maritime Safety Information broadcast, and no charted exclusion zone.
[...]
Port‑side and coastal impacts underline the breadth of exposure. Jebel Ali recorded a debris‑related berth fire following an aerial interception, and anchorage density has increased at both approaches to the Strait; JMIC warns that clustering raises secondary risks – restricted manoeuvring space, anchor dragging, and collision exposure. Electronic warfare effects amplify those hazards: GNSS interference, AIS anomalies, and congested/contradictory VHF are widespread across the Gulf, the Gulf of Oman and Hormuz approaches.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman region has experienced confirmed attacks on multiple merchant vessels, marking a shift from warnings to tangible damage (e.g., SKYLIGHT hit with injuries, MKD VYOM struck, SEA LA DONNA under investigation).
- The threat level is escalated: JMIC now cites CRITICAL risk with near-inevitable attack, and Dryad Global notes that sea mines remain a credible escalation option, though no mines have been sighted or confirmed as of writing.
- Operational guidance remains operator-led: ships shelter near approaches, reduce exposure, and consider routing changes to avoid chokepoints when possible.
- Official advisories remain in force:
  - US MARAD Maritime Alert 2026-001A: avoid wider Hormuz/Gulf area where possible; keep 30+ nm clear of US naval units; maintain NCAGS contact and monitor official outputs.
  - UKMTO advisories: emphasize caution, awareness of GNSS/AIS/VHF interference, and maintain listening watch on VHF 16; promptly report abnormal activity.
- Practical implications for watchstanders:
  - Anticipate short-notice traffic control, possible speed reductions, and route sanitisation.
  - Favor re-routing over extended holding near chokepoints.
  - If sheltering, widen CPA margins and maintain disciplined radio communications amid GNSS/VHF interference and naval proximity.
  - Preserve evidence (logs, ECDIS/VDR GNSS anomalies, message receipts) and maintain robust UKMTO/NCAGS reporting.

In short, the Strait of Hormuz watchstanding context is one of heightened risk, active incidents, and emphasis on vigilant navigation, interference awareness, and rapid decision-making to minimize exposure.

results.title: Iran Strait of Hormuz Risk Advisory March 2026 - Maritime News
results.text:
Iran Strait of Hormuz Risk Advisory March 2026 - Maritime News

# Iran's Maritime Risk Increases; Strait of Hormuz Traffic Near Halt Amid Advisories

US MARAD, UKMTO and JMIC maritime advisories warn of high risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman following over 20 confirmed incidents involving commercial vessels since late February. Transit volumes remain severely diminished while operators should coordinate with NCAGS while keeping a distance from naval units.

Maritime operators face heightened risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman as advisories from major authorities underscore ongoing disruptions to commercial shipping. Between March 8 and March 20, 2026, reports confirmed more than 20 incidents involving vessels and offshore facilities, contributing to near-paralyzed transit through key chokepoints.

## US MARAD Issues Advisory 2026-004 on March 16

The US Maritime Administration released Advisory 2026-004 on March 16, reinforcing high risk of attacks against commercial shipping in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. Masters are instructed to maintain at least 30 nautical miles from US naval units, cooperate with
highlights:
US MARAD, UKMTO and JMIC maritime advisories warn of high risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman following over 20 confirmed incidents involving commercial vessels since late February. Transit volumes remain severely diminished while operators should coordinate with NCAGS while keeping a distance from naval units.
[...]
Maritime operators face heightened risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman as advisories from major authorities underscore ongoing disruptions to commercial shipping. Between March 8 and March 20, 2026, reports confirmed more than 20 incidents involving vessels and offshore facilities, contributing to near-paralyzed transit through key chokepoints.
[...]
The US Maritime Administration released Advisory 2026-004 on March 16, reinforcing high risk of attacks against commercial shipping in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. Masters are instructed to maintain at least 30 nautical miles from US naval units, cooperate with coalition queries, and coordinate transits with NAVCENT NCAGS. The advisory warns against complying with over-the-horizon VHF or email instructions from Iranian forces, advising vessels to log encounters and continue on track.
[...]
The Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC), via UKMTO, elevated the maritime threat level to critical across the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. More than 20 incidents have occurred since February 28 escalations, including attacks on vessels, navigation system interference, and risks to port infrastructure. The risk of further disruptions remains high.
[...]
UKMTO has reported over 20 maritime incidents in and around the Strait since March 1. Operators are urged to monitor VHF 16, report abnormal activity, and stay aware of electronic interference and misidentification risks.
[...]
Commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains extremely limited. On March 15, only three outbound crossings were recorded, with no inbound transits, marking continued suppression far below normal levels. Maritime activity across the Gulf stayed heavily disrupted, distorting cargo flows and energy shipments.
[...]
Since the conflict's onset, 20 confirmed incidents targeted commercial vessels and offshore infrastructure, primarily cargo ships: 40 percent bulk carriers, 30 percent tankers, 20 percent container vessels, and 10 percent service vessels. About 45 percent of affected vessels had recent UAE port calls, with others from Iraq, Thailand, Vietnam, and Brazil.
[...]
, MARAD, and flag directives. Dual-theater risks combine Hormuz with Red Sea exposures, prompting route redist
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz and surrounding Persian Gulf are under elevated maritime security risk through March 2026, with UKMTO, JMIC, and US MARAD warnings of ongoing incidents and disruption to commercial traffic.
- More than 20 confirmed incidents (attacks, navigation system interference, and offshore facility risks) have occurred since late February, drastically reducing transit volumes; on March 15, outbound crossings through Hormuz were few, with no inbound transits recorded.
- Key advisories urge watchstandings to maintain distance from naval units (at least 30 nm from US vessels), cooperate with coalition NCAGS, monitor VHF Channel 16, log encounters, and avoid over-the-horizon instructions from Iranian forces. Be prepared for GNSS spoofing/jamming and electronic interference.
- Threat level is described as critical for the region, with heightened risk of further disruptions to vessels, ports, and energy shipments; operators should plan with strict security measures, heightened situational awareness, and potential routing/port alternatives.
- Operational considerations for watchstanders: monitor for mine threats, UAV/USV activity, GPS disruption, and possible mis-identification; observe port security restrictions (e.g., Iranian-registered cargos restricted in some ports), and maintain conservative routing anchored by NCAGS guidance.

results.title: Hormuz Shipping Traffic Grinds to a Halt as Tensions Deepen
results.text:
Hormuz Shipping Traffic Grinds to a Halt as Tensions Deepen

- Hormuz Shipping Traffic Grinds to a Halt as Tensions Deepen

# Hormuz Shipping Traffic Grinds to a Halt as Tensions Deepen

By Bloomberg

Apr 19, 2026

Photographer: Asghar Besharati/AP Photo

(Bloomberg) -- Observed transits of commercial ships through the Strait of Hormuz have come to a halt following a brief surge on Saturday as tensions ratcheted higher after vessels came under gunfire in the waterway and Iran warned against crossings.

No crossings were seen on Sunday, according to tracking data compiled by Bloomberg as of early afternoon in London. At least 13 oil tankers turned back toward the Gulf on Saturday, abandoning attempts to leave that began after Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi announced a day earlier that the strait was open.

But Tehran again shut the waterway following a refusal by the US to lift its own naval blockade of Iran’s ve

results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global oil and LNG, is experiencing heightened security disruption as tensions involving Iran escalate.
- After gunfire incidents and Iran’s warnings, observed ship transits have effectively halted, with no crossings seen on Sunday and several vessels reversing outbound attempts.
- The disruption follows Iran’s claim that the strait would be open during a ceasefire period, then reversal after the US Navy blockade stance, leaving millions of barrels of oil and LNG stranded in the Gulf.
- Overnight, limited outbound movements occurred (including at least four tankers, some linked to Iran or sanctioned by the US), while inbound flows diminished; several vessels attempted to navigate around Hormuz or divert toward UAE/Oman routes but faced блокades and re-routing.
- The situation created incentives for ships to switch off AIS/tracking to avoid detection, complicating accurate traffic assessments and potentially delaying global energy shipments.
- For watchstanding: expect heightened risk of sudden closures, armed-merchant-activity, and rapid shifts in transit viability; maintain real-time AIS monitoring, corroborate with UKMTO/autorities, prepare for abrupt rerouting and potential supply shocks in oil/LNG markets.

results.title: Hormuz Ceasefire Week One Maritime Intelligence Update
results.author: Maya Romi
results.text:
Hormuz Ceasefire Week One Maritime Intelligence Update

Apr 16, 2026 23 min read

# One Week Into the Ceasefire: A Maritime Intelligence Breakdown

#### What’s inside?

## At a Glance

- One week after the [ceasefire announcement](https://www.whitehouse.gov/releases/2026/04/peace-through-strength-operation-epic-fury-crushes-iranian-threat-as-ceasefire-takes-hold/), the Strait of Hormuz has not reopened to normal commercial traffic.
- Transit continues, but under controlled, selective, and inconsistent conditions shaped first by Iranian routing control and then by U.S. enforcement.
- The risk profile has not normalized, as blue-chip operators and major oil majors remain absent, while sanctioned, [falsely flagged](https://windward.ai/glossary/what-is-a-false-flag/), and risk-tolerant vessels continue to dominate activity.
- Gulf trade flows have structurally adapted through diversion networks centered on Salalah, Sohar, Khor Fakkan, Fujairah, and Jebel Ali.
- Iranian exports remain active, supported by [dark activity](https://windward.ai/glossary/dark-activity/), [spoofing](https://windward.ai/glossary/what-is-ais-spoofing/), and sustained loading at Kharg Island.
- [U.S. mine cleara
highlights:
That shifted as U.S. enforcement was introduced. Following the announcement and subsequent implementation of the blockade, vessel behavior became more fragmented, with turnarounds, delays, dark activity, and selective continuation replacing the earlier, more structured transit pattern.
[...]
It became even clearer once the U.S. blockade took effect on April 13. Sanctioned and falsely flagged vessels continued to transit, while others turned around, slowed, drifted, or went dark.
[...]
RICH STARRY (IMO: 9773301), a sanctioned tanker carrying Iranian cargo, abandoned an outbound transit shortly before the blockade came into effect, resumed movement hours later, and then turned around again after nearly clearing the Strait, indicating active decision-making to avoid enforcement exposure. In parallel, other vessels abandoned inbound transits, drifted in the Gulf of Oman, or continued movement while hugging Iranian territorial waters, reflecting attempts to reduce exposure while maintaining limited access.
[...]
. A 14
[...]
bunker in Fujairah,
[...]
By April 13, total Iranian oil-on-water stood at approximately 157.7 million barrels, with 97.6% destined for China. Remote Sensing Intelligence also identified two non-AIS transmitting VLCCs loading at Kharg Island, with an estimated combined capacity of approximately 4 million barrels, reinforcing the continued role of dark activity in supporting export continuity.
[...]
This activity confirms that Iranian exports remain structurally intact, supported by dark fleet operations and increasingly reliant on deceptive shipping practices and reduced-visibility practices, despite increasing external pressure.
[...]
operators remain absent, insurance constraints remain binding, and vessel participation remains narrow
[...]
At the same time, the ceasefire has not reversed the structural adjustments built during the conflict. Gulf trade flows remain reorganized through Oman and the UAE’s east coast. Alternative routing continues to extend voyage time and cost. Iranian exports remain active through dark and reduced-visibility operations. U.S. mine clearance and blockade measures have added a second enforcement layer rather than replacing the first.
[...]
At a Glance Operational Overview Maritime activity across the Strait of Hormuz remains stable in volume but increasingly uneven in behavior, with full AIS visibility during transit contrasted by a sharp rise in deceptive activity across the broader Gulf. On April 28, transit volumes held steady, with all crossings conducted while transmitting AIS. At the
[...]
Maritime activity across the
[...]
transit activity slowed again on
results.summary:
Summary:
- One week into the Hormuz ceasefire, the Strait of Hormuz has not reverted to open navigation. Transit continues but under a constrained, uneven regime defined first by Iranian routing control and then by U.S. enforcement.
- The security landscape remains elevated and uncertain: blue‑chip operators and major oil majors remain absent, while sanctioned, falsely flagged, and risk-tolerant vessels dominate activity.
- Access is mediated through specific corridors and is subject to approval, warnings, toll considerations, and ongoing inspections, with no unified legal framework in place.
- A two-layer security regime emerged: Iranian routing/control persists, and U.S. mine clearance and naval blockade measures add a second enforcement layer that actively shapes vessel behavior.
- Trade flows have adapted to the security regime, with diversion networks centered on Salalah, Sohar, Khor Fakkan, Fujairah, and Jebel Ali becoming nodes of activity.
- Iranian exports remain active, aided by covert/opaque activity (dark activity), spoofing, and continued loading at Kharg Island.
- Overall: after week one, the Hormuz ceasefire created a more complex maritime security environment—still controllable by Iran but increasingly influenced by U.S. maritime enforcement—rather than a return to normal commercial navigation.

results.title: Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation
results.author: Dimitra Sotirchos
results.text:
Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation 

By Saleem Khan, Chief Data & Analytics Officer – Pole Star Global

Since 5 March, Pole Star Global has tracked 3,396 vessels in and around the Strait of Hormuz. Roughly 1 in 15 of them – 231 ships – are exhibiting suspicious positional anomalies. “Jumping” implausible distances, going briefly “ghosted,” or reappearing in patterns consistent with GPS and AIS interference. This is no longer a story about isolated glitches; it’s a systematic, geographically clustered distortion of reality at sea.

At the same time, we are seeing a near-total cessation of commercial traffic through the Strait, and some ships leaving the region are broadcasting extraordinary “destinations” such as “China Owner and All Crew”, turning what should be a navigational field into a live political message: don’t shoot, we’re not your target.

Beyond the chokepoint, about 540 oil tankers carrying an estimated 314 million barrels are currently at sea with no fixed destination, listed simply as “awaiting orders” – effectively a $30 billion floating oil exchange waiting for a safe route and a safe buyer.

Pole Star Global fuses AIS, satellite detections, ownership,
highlights:
Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation 

By Saleem Khan, Chief Data & Analytics Officer – Pole Star Global
[...]
Since 5 March, Pole Star Global has tracked 3,396 vessels in and around the Strait of Hormuz. Roughly 1 in 15 of them – 231 ships – are exhibiting suspicious positional anomalies. “Jumping” implausible distances, going briefly “ghosted,” or reappearing in patterns consistent with GPS and AIS interference. This is no longer a story about isolated glitches; it’s a systematic, geographically clustered distortion of reality at sea.
[...]
At the same time, we are seeing a near-total cessation of commercial traffic through the Strait, and some ships leaving the region are broadcasting extraordinary “destinations” such as “China Owner and All Crew”, turning what should be a navigational field into a live political message: don’t shoot, we’re not your target.
[...]
Beyond the chokepoint, about 540 oil tankers carrying an estimated 314 million barrels are currently at sea with no fixed destination, listed simply as “awaiting orders” – effectively a $30 billion floating oil exchange waiting for a safe route and a safe buyer.
[...]
Pole Star Global
[...]
analytics to help governments
[...]
from manipulation. Our data offers an unusually
[...]
how conflict,
[...]
, and digital
[...]
are reshaping global trade in
[...]
The image below shows intentional AIS manipulation by a vessel called the NV Aquamarine (in red trails). This vessel was travelling at an astounding 102 knots (188 km/hr) from the Strait to the Iran/Iraq border. This is impossible for a tanker that has a capacity to carry 350,000 barrels of oil worth $35M at current spot rates.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz is experiencing widespread GPS spoofing and AIS manipulation, with Pole Star Global recording 231 suspicious vessels (~1 in 15) out of 3,396 analyzed, including implausible movements, ghosting, and destination spoofing.
- These anomalies indicate systematic, geographically clustered digital deception, not isolated glitches, coinciding with a near-cessation of commercial traffic through the strait.
- Some ships that depart the region broadcast provocative destinations (e.g., “China Owner and All Crew”), signaling political messaging alongside navigational disruption.
- Beyond the chokepoint, roughly 540 oil tankers with about 314 million barrels are at sea with no fixed destination, described as “awaiting orders,” effectively creating a volatile floating oil market worth billions.
- Pole Star Global emphasizes the need to distinguish malfunction from manipulation using integrated AIS, satellite detections, ownership, and behavioral analytics to inform governments, insurers, and shipping companies.
- The incident complicates maritime security, signaling risks of coercion, conflict spillover, and potential disruption to global energy trade; AIS interference is a component of a broader information and security challenge in the region.

results.title: Container ships stranded near Strait of Hormuz as AIS disruptions surge | Global Maritime Hub
results.author: Editor
results.text:
Container ships stranded near Strait of Hormuz as AIS disruptions surge | Global Maritime Hub 

- [Login](https://globalmaritimehub.com/my-account)
- [Shipping Trends](https://globalmaritimehub.com/category/shipping-trends)

# Container ships stranded near Strait of Hormuz as AIS disruptions surge

- [Editor](https://globalmaritimehub.com/author/david)
- March 12, 2026

Container ships around the Strait of Hormuz are experiencing unprecedented AIS signal disruptions, according to industry analysts Alphaliner.

Alphaliner live satellite vessel tracking shows that 48 container ships in the waters west of the Strait of Hormuz currently have their transponders switched off or are broadcasting spoofed positions, as of 10 March.

Disruption in the area reached even higher levels earlier in the month. A record 65 container ships registered lost or manipulated signals on 4 March, when the number of vessels with visible and disrupted transmissions was split exactly in half.

Abnormally high levels of signal disruption in the region persisted over the following days, with 62 ships recorded on 6 March, 63 on 7 March, and 60 on 8 March.

For comparison, during the June 2025 Twelve-Day War, the
highlights:
Container ships stranded near Strait
[...]
Container ships around the Strait of Hormuz are experiencing unprecedented AIS signal disruptions, according to industry analysts Alphaliner.
[...]
Alphaliner live satellite vessel tracking shows that 48 container ships in the waters west of the Strait of Hormuz currently have their transponders switched off or are broadcasting spoofed positions, as of 10 March.
[...]
Disruption in the area reached even higher levels earlier in the month. A record 65 container ships registered lost or manipulated signals on 4 March, when the number of vessels with visible and disrupted transmissions was split exactly in half.
[...]
Abnormally high levels of signal disruption in the region persisted over the following days, with 62 ships recorded on 6 March, 63 on 7 March, and 60 on 8 March.
[...]
For comparison, during the June 2025 Twelve-Day War, the number of container ships around Hormuz operating with disrupted signals peaked at 32 vessels.
[...]
The last container ship recorded leaving the Persian (Arabian) Gulf via the Strait of Hormuz was observed on 5 March, and was a sub-3,000 TEU vessel. Since then, no additional container ships have exited the Gulf.
[...]
In total, 131 container ships remain stranded west of the Strait of Hormuz, according to live vessel monitoring.
[...]
### Strait of Horm
[...]
cargo bookings resume for Gulf states
[...]
### Strait of Hormuz cargo bookings resume for Gulf states
results.summary:
Summary:
- The article reports widespread AIS (Automatic Identification System) disruptions for container ships near the Strait of Hormuz, with vessels either turning off transponders or broadcasting spoofed positions.
- Key figures from Alphaliner live tracking: 65 ships affected on 4 March (peak), 62 on 6 March, 63 on 7 March, 60 on 8 March; 48 ships currently disrupted as of 10 March; total 131 ships stranded west of Hormuz.
- Disruptions are higher than observed during prior Gulf incidents (e.g., June 2025 Twelve-Day War peaked at 32 vessels).
- Last recorded exit of a container ship from the Persian Gulf via the Strait of Hormuz was 5 March; since then, no ships have exited, indicating a curtailment in traffic and increased risk exposure for regional routing.
- Source attribution: Alphaliner; implications discussed in the context of AIS manipulation and vessel tracking integrity.

Relevance to Strait of Hormuz watchstanding:
- Elevated risk to maritime domain awareness due to intentional AIS signal manipulation, reducing reliability of vessel tracking and collision avoidance.
- Potential for misrepresentation of vessel positions complicates threat assessment, convoy planning, and interdiction or response readiness in a sensitive chokepoint.
- Heightened probability of security incidents, piracy, or misrouting as ships seek safer corridors or wait for guidance amid partial network visibility.
- Indicates a need for redundant surveillance methods (radar, satellite imagery, ISR feeds) and enhanced coordination with regional partners to maintain situational picture.
- Operational impact: reduced throughput and potential for supply-chain disruption, necessitating tighter watchstanding discipline, fuel/crew planning, and contingency routing analysis.

If you want, I can tailor this into a briefing slide with risk ratings (likelihood/impact), recommended watchstanding actions, and monitoring cues.

results.author: Sky News      Tue, 10 March 2026 at 3:14 pm CET   5 min read
results.text:
Dark ships and shadow fleets – what is crossing 'closed' Strait of Hormuz?

# Dark ships and shadow fleets – what is crossing 'closed' Strait of Hormuz?

Iran's move to shut the Strait of Hormuz has brought shipping through the 24-mile stretch of water close to a standstill.

Only a handful of vessels have transited in recent days, many switching off their tracking systems or linked to "shadow fleets".

"They can't physically close a waterway of that size, but the threat is there", said Richard Meade, editor-in-chief of maritime risk company Lloyd's List Intelligence.

Mr Meade said Iran has a proven history of using everything from ballistic missiles to unmanned aerial and seaborne drones.

What is making it through?

Sky News' Data & Forensics team has focused on 13 ships that have transited the Strait of Hormuz between 2 and 9 March.

We know the real number of crossings is much higher, as some ships turn their tracking system off, a practice known as going "dark".

Data from marine analytics firm IMF Portwatch suggests that in normal times,
highlights:
Iran's move to shut the Strait of Hormuz has brought shipping through the 24-mile stretch of water close to a standstill.
[...]
Only a handful of vessels have transited in recent days, many switching off their tracking systems or linked to "shadow fleets".
[...]
We know the real number of crossings is much higher, as some ships turn their tracking system off, a practice known as going "dark".
[...]
Of the ships we have identified passing through the Strait of Hormuz, many have links to Iran, China or Russia. There are also ships from Greece, India, the UAE and Singapore.
[...]
Shadow fleet tankers dominate current crossings of the Strait of Hormuz, according to Lloyd's List Intelligence.
[...]
They found that, of the 13 large oil and gas carriers that crossed between 2-9 March, eight were classified as part of the so-called shadow fleet.
[...]
Thirteen vessels in or near the Strait of Hormuz have been attacked since Iran blocked the waterway, according to the International Maritime Organisation (IMO).
[...]
According to United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO), a total of 17 incidents from 28 February to 11 March have affected vessels operating in and around the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman.
[...]
A few hours later, in the Straight of Hormuz close to Oman, a security officer reported their cargo ship was hit by an unknown projectile which resulted in a fire onboard. The vessel requested assistance and the crew evacuated.
[...]
The ship was 30 nautical miles southeast of the Kuwait coast. UKMTO reported an oil spill because of the incident.
[...]
There has been a huge surge in GPS jamming in the region. Hundreds of ships jump around the map and then cluster in very small areas.
[...]
GPS ship jamming is
[...]
, causing ships
[...]
Tracking data from MarineTraffic shows container ship 'Maersk Cincinnati' rerouting its course away from the strait.
[...]
On 2 March, the data shows the ship did an almost U-turn at around 2pm UTC. It continued to retreat from the area until 4 March, finally returning to the Port of Salalah in Oman, heading to the Gulf of Kutch on 10 March, where it appeared "laded", heavily loaded.
[...]
Mr Meade said: "We're seeing a number of ships doing U-turns. They're likely getting orders to go and perform alternative operations. Now, that's fine for the ships that are headed into the Gulf, but for the ships that are already there, they're essentially stuck."
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz, a 24-mile-wide chokepoint, has seen near standstill traffic after Iran threatened to shut it, raising maritime security concerns for watchstanders.
- Attacks and “dark” transits (ships turning off tracking) have increased the perceived risk to vessels in the area, prompting heightened vigilance by crews and authorities.
- A small number of ships have continued to transit (13 identified between March 2–9; many more likely moving while “dark”), with several linked to Iran, Russia, or other states, and some associated with the so-called shadow fleet carrying sanctioned cargo.
- Shadow fleet vessels—oil tankers connected to sanctioned cargo—dominated recent crossings, complicating tracking and risk assessment for watchstanders.
- Incidents: at least 17 reported incidents affecting vessels in and around the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman from Feb 28–Mar 11, including multiple projectile hits and fires; several crew were injured or killed early March.
- Security implications for watchstanders:
  - Increased need to monitor for suspicious transit patterns, including dark-ship activity and sanctioned cargo movements.
  - Heightened readiness to respond to suspected attacks or projectile incidents, with coordination among UKMTO, IMSO, and other authorities.
  - Importance of corroborating AIS data, implementing convoy or risk-based routing where possible, and maintaining robust threat intelligence on vessel flags, owners, and cargoes.
  - Need to anticipate potential escalations tied to political developments, as adversaries may leverage the chokepoint to exert pressure or disrupt global oil flows.
- Practical watchstanding takeaways:
  - Elevate monitoring of strait approaches for anomalies in ship behavior, speed, or routing.
  - Prepare for rapid casualty, firefighting, and damage-control responses in case of incidents.
  - Maintain updated advisories and avoid ambiguous or “dark” transits when risk indicators rise.
  - Coordinate with international agencies and commercial partners to share incident reports and vessel-level risk assessments.

results.title: Hormuz Active Crisis 5 Ships Hit Traffic Collapses | gCaptain
results.author: Mike Schuler
results.text:
Hormuz Active Crisis 5 Ships Hit Traffic Collapses | gCaptain

Oil tankers pass through the Strait of Hormuz, December 21, 2018. REUTERS/Hamad I Mohammed

# Strait of Hormuz Enters Active Crisis: Five Ships Hit, Traffic Collapses, Tanker Markets Brace for Historic Shock

The Strait of Hormuz has entered its most dangerous phase in years after a concentrated wave of missile and drone attacks struck five commercial vessels across the Gulf of Oman and UAE waters. With ports temporarily suspending operations, insurers reassessing war-risk exposure, and AIS data showing transits collapsing, the region’s maritime security environment has shifted from elevated tension to active crisis.

As of Sunday, the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) has [elevated](https://www.ukmto.org/-/media/ukmto/products/update-002---001---jmic-advisory-note-28_feb_2026_final.pdf?rev=54f3ebd757e14bccb7e6ac9b62108bcb) the regional maritime threat level to CRITICAL after confirmed missile and drone attacks against multiple commercial vessels in the Gulf of Oman, the Musandam approaches, and UAE coastal waters — a designation indicating an attack is considered almost certain.

In just 24 hours, five vessels w
highlights:
The Strait of Hormuz has entered its most dangerous phase in years after a concentrated wave of missile and drone attacks struck five commercial vessels across the Gulf of Oman and UAE waters. With ports temporarily suspending operations, insurers reassessing war-risk exposure, and AIS data showing transits collapsing, the region’s maritime security environment has shifted from elevated tension to active crisis.
[...]
As of Sunday, the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) has elevated the regional maritime threat level to CRITICAL after confirmed missile and drone attacks against multiple commercial vessels in the Gulf of Oman, the Musandam approaches, and UAE coastal waters — a designation indicating an attack is considered almost certain.
[...]

[...]

[...]

[...]
of Mina Saqr,
[...]

[...]
Stand-off missile and
[...]
across the Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman. There is no confirmed sea mine
[...]
- Jebel Ali briefly suspended operations following an aerial interception that caused a fire before resuming limited activity.
- Duqm Port in Oman remains operational after two UAV strikes injured one worker.
- Ras Tanura Refinery in Saudi Arabia was reportedly shut following a drone strike and fire.
- Bahrain temporarily suspended port operations and pilotage services.
- Qatar’s Ras Laffan and Mesaieed ports reported significant GPS signal degradation.
[...]
Significant GNSS/GPS interference is being reported across the Strait’s approaches, compounding navigational risks amid heavy congestion and heightened aerial threats.
[...]
AIS tracking indicates transits through the Strait may have fallen to just 28 vessels over a 24-hour period — roughly an 80% decline from the historical daily average near 138 vessels.
[...]
Cargo transits dropped from 98 combined eastbound/westbound movements on February 28 to just 18 on March 1. Tanker movements fell even more sharply, from 50 combined transits to just three.
[...]
JMIC cautioned the drop may reflect a short-term pause rather than a structural halt, but insurers and operators are clearly reassessing risk.
[...]
“Insurance availability may now act as a primary gating factor for transit decisions independent of formal navigational closure,” JMIC noted.
[...]
Even absent an official blockade, the combination of aerial threat, port suspensions, insurance withdrawals, and GPS disruption is effectively constraining commercialadfad movement.
results.summary:
Summary:
The Strait of Hormuz has entered an active crisis with missile and drone attacks targeting five vessels in the Gulf of Oman and UAE waters. Maritime security relevance for watchstanding includes:

- Elevated threat level: JMIC has raised regional threat to CRITICAL, signaling near-inevitable attacks and heightened risk to commercial traffic.
- Widespread risk to all vessels: Advisory notes that all merchant ships, regardless of flag, are at risk; no single actor link established. Vigilance required for every transit.
- Operational disruptions: Multiple incidents caused port suspensions and reduced throughput (e.g., Jebel Ali brief shutdown, Bahrain disruptions), with potential knock-on effects for supply chains and insurance.
- Navigation and GPS/GNSS interference: Reports of significant GPS signal degradation and GNSS interference complicate routing, situational awareness, and collision avoidance in a congested corridor.
- Cargo and crew risk: Attacks produced casualties and injuries; safety procedures and contingency planning for crew welfare, evacuations, and emergency response are critical.
- Infrastructure exposure: Attacks extended to energy and port infrastructure (Duqm, Ras Tanura, Ras Laffan, Mesaieed), increasing the likelihood of escalation and export disruption.
- Watchstanding implications:
  - Increase ISR overlays: integrate real-time AIS, radar, EW, and sensor data; anticipate sudden transits or vessel evasive actions.
  - Strict risk assessments: assume elevated threat profiles for all commercial ships; review speed, routing, and anchorage plans.
  - Communications discipline: prepare for degraded GNSS-dependent navigation; rely on alternative navigation aids and preplanned contingencies.
  - Coordination and readiness: maintain contact with regional authorities (UKMTO/JMIC) and industry partners; implement robust emergency response and vessel recovery procedures.
  - Insurance and ops planning: expect reassessments of war-risk exposure, premium adjustments, and potential changes to routing around higher-risk segments.

In short, the current environment shifts from heightened tension to an active crisis with genuine, near-term risk to transit safety, requiring enhanced vigilance, resilient navigation, and rapid interagency coordination for Hormuz watchstanders.

results.title: The Strait of Hormuz is a data problem, not just a military one - Iran
results.text:
The Strait of Hormuz is a data problem, not just a military one - Iran

# The Strait of Hormuz is a data problem, not just a military one

Since the first tanker pushed through it, the Strait of Hormuz has been treated as a static math problem. You tallied the hulls, weighed the warheads and assumed you knew the score. If you could map the Fifth Fleet’s tonnage against the IRGC’s mine density, you had a working theory on who held the leverage and what a barrel of crude ought to cost. For decades, we looked at those 21 miles of water and saw a cage made of steel.

That logic is now an artifact. The “grey hull” era of deterrence didn’t end with a kinetic explosion. It just quietly stopped being the thing that mattered. What’s happening in the Gulf isn’t a traditional naval confrontation. It’s the violent, accelerating breakdown of a global system that destroyers aren’t equipped to target.

[A note on sourcing: Several of the data points below come from Windward, whose CEO co-authored this piece, and from the maritime data sector in which co-author Erik Bethel’s firm, Mare Liberum, is an active investor. We have flagged these instances and stand behind the underlying figures, which ar
highlights:
Run the numbers. When U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran kicked off on February 28, traffic through the world’s most critical oil artery didn’t just slow — it cratered by 97% in a single week, according to Windward’s Q1 2026 shipping risk report. Upwards of 800 ships were left idling west of the chokepoint, effectively paralyzed. Of the 142.5 million barrels loaded in March, a staggering 128 million never cleared the gap. By late April, with the ceasefire fraying and tankers still taking hits mid-transit, the Strait is, in the authors’ assessment, closed to commercial traffic.
[...]
The missiles and drones make for good headlines, but they’re a distraction. The real story is that the Strait has gone dark. Not in some poetic sense, but literally. The Automatic Identification System (AIS) — the network that’s supposed to be the “gold standard” for commercial tracking — has stopped telling the truth.
[...]
Ships are vanishing into digital black holes only to materialize hours later on the other side of Hormuz with the transit completed in total silence. On April 21, Windward’s platform identified 296 vessels off Bandar Abbas. Of these, only 74 were transmitting AIS signals — a cooperative rate of roughly 25%.
[...]
Others are caught in the crossfire of GPS spoofing attacks — fake satellite signals, broadcast from shore, that fool a ship’s navigation into thinking it’s somewhere it isn’t. The result is a fleet of tankers whose screens show them circling inland airports or drifting across the Iranian desert. Windward identified at least 30 jamming clusters across Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, the UAE, Qatar, Oman and Iran. Some have gone further, broadcasting the identity numbers of hulls that were scrapped years ago. These are zombie ships — very real tankers operating under the digital signatures of vessels that no longer exist.
[...]
Data suggest AIS is now underreporting Hormuz traffic by half. In Q1 alone, nearly a million GPS jamming incidents hit over 1,100 vessels. Satellite imagery recently caught seven VLCCs — 14 million barrels of capacity — off Iran’s coast with zero digital footprint. Iran claims 11 million barrels exported during a blockade where commercial feeds show a graveyard. Both are true. That is the problem.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The strategic risk in the Strait of Hormuz now centers on data integrity and information security, not solely on naval force projection.
- Recent disruptions show the vulnerability of AIS-based tracking: self-reported, easily spoofed signals have allowed ships to vanish or appear elsewhere, undermining transparency, insurance pricing, regulatory oversight, and market operations.
- GPS jamming and spoofing further erode reliable navigation and situational awareness, creating “zombie ships” and mislabelled vessel identities that complicate watchstanding, threat assessment, and response planning.
- The current security dynamic shifts from kinetic deterrence to adversaries degrading the information environment that underpins global shipping, risks to energy flows, and economic security.
- For watchstanders, key implications include: heightened need for corroborating intelligence sources beyond AIS, awareness of spoofing/jamming tactics, and prioritization of cyber/denial-of-service countermeasures and resilience in maritime domain awareness.
- Overall, safeguarding Strait of Hormuz security now requires integrated maritime data security, robust verification of vessel identity and position, and coordination across commercial, regulatory, and military stakeholders to counter the data-centric dimension of the threat.
